does: This paper systematically forecasts potential malicious uses of AI technologies and proposes prevention and mitigation strategies. Rather than speculating abstractly, the authors conduct sustained research across multiple threat categories, examining how adversaries might exploit AI capabilities for harmful purposes such as cyberattacks, disinformation, surveillance, or autonomous weapons. The work synthesizes insights from security experts, technologists, and policy specialists to develop concrete countermeasures, defensive frameworks, and governance approaches. The paper combines threat modeling with actionable recommendations for technical safeguards, institutional accountability, and international coordination to reduce risks from intentional misuse.
connects: This work builds on growing concern within the AI safety and security communities about dual-use risks and misalignment between capability development and safety measures. It extends earlier work on biosecurity and WMD risks by applying established threat-modeling methodologies to AI specifically. The paper engages with prior research on AI governance, technical alignment, and security vulnerabilities, while also responding to the limitations of purely academic discussions by bringing together practitioners from policy, cybersecurity, and civil liberties. It connects to nascent efforts in responsible disclosure and AI incident preparedness, drawing on frameworks developed for managing risks in other transformative technologies.
enables: This work provides a foundation for developing concrete AI security policies and institutional responses to malicious use cases. By mapping specific threat vectors and mitigation strategies, it enables policymakers, companies, and international bodies to prioritize defenses and allocate resources more effectively. The paper points toward future research on detection and attribution of AI-enabled attacks, development of technical controls for constraining AI systems, and strengthening norms around responsible AI development. It supports the emergence of AI-specific incident response frameworks, red-teaming methodologies for adversarial uses, and cross-sector coordination mechanisms. Ultimately, it positions principled prevention of malicious AI misuse as a central design challenge alongside traditional AI safety concerns.
